features.rationale: (must_be, 5.44): Core commodity function — every flowmeter quantifies flow rate. Expected in district energy metering. Primary drivers: Reliability (8) — no mechanical wear means stable long-term accuracy per EN 1434; Cost (7) — eliminates revenue leakage from calibration drift. Grounded in N01 (flow accuracy ±2%, imp=10) and N25 (field failure rate <0.1%, imp=9). [SRC: JTBD_steam]

(attractive, 6.00): Traditional district energy substations use separate flow sensor + temperature probes. Combined flow+temp in a single body is a genuine delighter — eliminates a component, reduces wiring, and ensures co-located measurement. Directly addresses N22 (single-point flow + temperature, opp=12). Primary drivers: Cost (8) — eliminates separate temp probe; Reliability (7) — one fewer failure point; Pain (7) — removes installation labor for second sensor. [SRC: JTBD_steam, 01_product_decomposition]

(one_dimensional, 2.89): Unobstructed bore avoids head-loss penalty. In district substations, the heat exchanger and control valve dominate pressure drop — the meter's contribution is marginal. One_dimensional rather than indifferent because hydraulic designers do value zero head-loss, but the magnitude of contribution is small. Primary driver: Cost (5) — marginal pump energy savings. [SRC: 01_product_decomposition]

(one_dimensional, 6.44): Strongest differentiator vs the incumbent mechanical impeller meter (40-50% market share). Eliminates magnetite seizure (N11, opp=15), calibration drift from bearing wear (N13, opp=15), and mandatory 5-6 year fleet replacement cycles (N12, opp=13). Primary drivers: Cost (9) — eliminates fleet-wide replacement cycle; Reliability (9) — eliminates all mechanical failure modes; Pain (8) — removes the entire maintenance scheduling burden across hundreds of substations; Confidence (8) — stable calibration over 15+ years builds billing trust. [SRC: JTBD_steam]

(attractive, 5.00): Inherent to transit-time principle. Critical for 4th/5th generation low-temperature prosumer networks where buildings inject excess heat back into the grid. Mechanical impeller meters cannot detect reverse flow. Genuine delighter for network operators transitioning to bidirectional operation. Primary drivers: Cost (6) — eliminates need for separate check valves or reverse-flow sensors; Delight (6) — "the meter handles bidirectional billing automatically" is genuinely surprising to legacy operators; Confidence (6) — accurate net-energy billing in prosumer networks. [SRC: JTBD_steam segment 6: 4th/5th gen low-temperature network]

(attractive, 6.44): Detects bubbles, empty pipes, glycol presence. Traditional meters have zero diagnostic capability — air entrainment silently biases billing for months (N07, opp=15). Distinguishing genuine zero-flow from transient signal anomalies prevents both false alarms and missed real failures (N17, opp=13). Primary drivers: Confidence (8) — operators know when measurement is valid vs compromised; Safety (7) — empty-pipe alarm and flow-stoppage discrimination; Cost (7) — prevents silent billing errors from undetected air. [SRC: JTBD_steam]

(must_be, 4.22): District heating supply temperatures swing 50-120°C seasonally (N03, opp=14). Speed of sound in water changes ~2.4 m/s per °C. Without compensation, error reaches ~1.5%. Must_be because any flow sensor deployed in a temperature-varying environment MUST compensate — absence would be disqualifying. Primary driver: Reliability (7) — reduces temperature-induced error from ~1.5% to ~0.2%. [SRC: 01_product_decomposition, JTBD_steam]
